evidence: Red/brown soil (Terra Rossa) is highly characteristic of the Paraná Basin, covering southern/central Brazil, eastern Paraguay, and Misiones (Argentina).
Double yellow center lines with white edge lines are the standard road marking configuration for Brazil and other Mercosur countries.
The presence of palms mixed with deciduous trees in a flat agricultural setting is typical of the Cerrado and Atlantic Forest transition zones in Mato Grosso do Sul, Paraná, and São Paulo (Brazil).
Lattice electricity pylons are common for high-voltage transmission across rural South America.
A white pickup truck camera car is a known vehicle used for Google Street View coverage in specific rural regions of Brazil (often Gen 3/4).
Decorative white fences or gates are common for 'Fazendas' (large farms) in rural Brazil.
contradictions: While Misiones (Argentina) has red soil, it is generally more hilly and tropical than the described flat agricultural land.
The United States has red soil in the Southeast (Georgia) and Oklahoma, but the combination with palms and a white pickup truck camera car is less diagnostic than in Brazil.
